title: Kurt Russell

infobox.value: Kurt Vogel Russell   (1951-03-17) March 17, 1951 (age 75)  [Springfield, Massachusetts](/wiki/Springfield,_Massachusetts "Springfield, Massachusetts"), U.S.
[Thousand Oaks High School](/wiki/Thousand_Oaks_High_School "Thousand Oaks High School")

(born March 17, 1951) is an American actor. He began his career as a child actor before transitioning to leading roles as an adult in various genres such as [action adventures](/wiki/Action_films "Action films"), science-fiction, [westerns](/wiki/Westerns "Westerns"), [romance films](/wiki/Romance_films "Romance films"), [comedic films](/wiki/Comedy_films "Comedy films"), and [family dramas](/wiki/Family_films "Family films"). He is known for collaborating with filmmakers such as [John Carpenter](/wiki/John_Carpenter "John Carpenter") and [Quentin Tarantino](/wiki/Quentin_Tarantino "Quentin Tarantino"), and has received a [Critics' Choice Super Award](/wiki/Critics%27_Choice_Super_Award "Critics' Choice Super Award") as well as various award nominations, including for a [Primetime Emmy Award](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award "Primetime Emmy Award") and a [Golden Globe Award](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award "Golden Globe Award").

**Kurt Vogel Russell** (born March 17, 1951) is an American actor. He began his career as a child actor before transitioning to leading roles as an adult in various genres such as [action adventures](/wiki/Action_films "Action films"), science-fiction, [westerns](/wiki/Westerns "Westerns"), [romance films](/wiki/Romance_films "Romance films"), [comedic films](/wiki/Comedy_films "Comedy films"), and [family dramas](/wiki/Family_films "Family films"). He is known for collaborating with filmmakers such as [John Carpenter](/wiki/John_Carpenter "John Carpenter") and [Quentin Tarantino](/wiki/Quentin_Tarantino "Quentin Tarantino"), and has received a [Critics' Choice Super Award](/wiki/Critics%27_Choice_Super_Award "Critics' Choice Super Award") as well as various award nominations, including for a [Primetime Emmy Award](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award "Primetime Emmy Award") and a [Golden Globe Award](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award "Golden Globe Award").

At the age of 12, he began acting in the [Western TV series](/wiki/Westerns_on_television "Westerns on television") *[The Travels of Jaimie McPheeters](/wiki/The_Travels_of_Jaimie_McPheeters_(TV_series) "The Travels of Jaimie McPheeters (TV series)")* (1963–1964). In the late 1960s, he signed a ten-year contract with [the Walt Disney Company](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company") starring in films such as *[The Computer Wore Tennis Shoes](/wiki/The_Computer_Wore_Tennis_Shoes "The Computer Wore Tennis Shoes")* (1969), *[Now You See Him, Now You Don't](/wiki/Now_You_See_Him,_Now_You_Don%27t "Now You See Him, Now You Don't")* (1972), and *[The Strongest Man in the World](/wiki/The_Strongest_Man_in_the_World "The Strongest Man in the World")* (1975). For his portrayal of [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll") [superstar](/wiki/Superstar "Superstar") [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley") in the television film *[Elvis](/wiki/Elvis_(1979_film) "Elvis (1979 film)")* (1979), he was nominated for the [Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Lead Actor in a Limited Series or Movie](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Lead_Actor_in_a_Limited_Series_or_Movie "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Lead Actor in a Limited Series or Movie").[[1]](#cite_note-Elvis-1)

Russell was nominated for a [Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor – Motion Picture](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor_%E2%80%93_Motion_Picture "Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor – Motion Picture") for his role in [Mike Nichols](/wiki/Mike_Nichols "Mike Nichols")'s *[Silkwood](/wiki/Silkwood "Silkwood")* (1983). He collaborated with director [John Carpenter](/wiki/John_Carpenter "John Carpenter") playing [antiheroes](/wiki/Antihero "Antihero") in the action films *[Escape from New York](/wiki/Escape_from_New_York "Escape from New York")* (1981) and its sequel *[Escape from L.A.](/wiki/Escape_from_L.A. "Escape from L.A.")* (1996), the horror film *[The Thing](/wiki/The_Thing_(1982_film) "The Thing (1982 film)")* (1982), and the comedy action film *[Big Trouble in Little China](/wiki/Big_Trouble_in_Little_China "Big Trouble in Little China")* (1986). Russell also acted in *[Used Cars](/wiki/Used_Cars "Used Cars")* (1980), *[The Fox and the Hound](/wiki/The_Fox_and_the_Hound "The Fox and the Hound")* (1981), *[The Best of Times](/wiki/The_Best_of_Times_(1986_film) "The Best of Times (1986 film)")* (1986), *[Overboard](/wiki/Overboard_(1987_film) "Overboard (1987 film)")* (1987), *[Backdraft](/wiki/Backdraft_(film) "Backdraft (film)")* (1991), *[Tombstone](/wiki/Tombstone_(film) "Tombstone (film)")* (1993), *[Stargate](/wiki/Stargate_(film) "Stargate (film)")* (1994), *[Vanilla Sky](/wiki/Vanilla_Sky "Vanilla Sky")* (2001), *[Miracle](/wiki/Miracle_(2004_film) "Miracle (2004 film)")* (2004), *[Dreamer](/wiki/Dreamer_(2005_film) "Dreamer (2005 film)")*, *[Sky High](/wiki/Sky_High_(2005_film) "Sky High (2005 film)")* (both 2005), *[Death Proof](/wiki/Death_Proof "Death Proof")* (2007), *[The Hateful Eight](/wiki/The_Hateful_Eight "The Hateful Eight")* (2015), and *[Once Upon a Time in Hollywood](/wiki/Once_Upon_a_Time_in_Hollywood "Once Upon a Time in Hollywood")* (2019).

Russell has also acted in several franchises, portraying Mr. Nobody in three films of the *[Fast & Furious](/wiki/Fast_%26_Furious "Fast & Furious")* franchise from 2015 to 2021, [Ego](/wiki/Ego_the_Living_Planet "Ego the Living Planet") in the [Marvel Cinematic Universe](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe "Marvel Cinematic Universe") (MCU) installments *[Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2 "Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2")* (2017) and *[What If...?](/wiki/What_If...%3F_(TV_series) "What If...? (TV series)")* (2021), and [Santa Claus](/wiki/Santa_Claus "Santa Claus") in the [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") films *[The Christmas Chronicles](/wiki/The_Christmas_Chronicles "The Christmas Chronicles")* (2018) and *[The Christmas Chronicles 2](/wiki/The_Christmas_Chronicles_2 "The Christmas Chronicles 2")* (2020).[[2]](#cite_note-Taylor-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kurt_Russell&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Kurt Vogel Russell was born on March 17, 1951,[[4]](#cite_note-4) at [Wesson Maternity Hospital](/wiki/Baystate_Health "Baystate Health") in [Springfield, Massachusetts](/wiki/Springfield,_Massachusetts "Springfield, Massachusetts").[[5]](#cite_note-5) His father, [Bing Russell](/wiki/Bing_Russell "Bing Russell"), was also an actor. His mother, Louise Julia ([née](/wiki/Birth_name#Maiden_and_married_names "Birth name") Crone) Russell,[[6]](#cite_note-6) was a dancer.[[7]](#cite_note-auto5-7) Russell is of English, Irish, German, and Scottish ancestry.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] He has three sisters,[[7]](#cite_note-auto5-7) Jill Franco,[[8]](#cite_note-8) Jamie and Jody.[[7]](#cite_note-auto5-7) His family relocated to California when he was a child, and Russell grew up in [Thousand Oaks](/wiki/Thousand_Oaks "Thousand Oaks").[[9]](#cite_note-biography-9) Russell played little league baseball throughout his grade school years[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) and also on his high school baseball team as a second baseman.[[12]](#cite_note-12) He graduated from [Thousand Oaks High School](/wiki/Thousand_Oaks_High_School "Thousand Oaks High School") in California in 1969.[[13]](#cite_note-13) He signed a minor league contract in 1971 and played two seasons in the Class A Northwest League where he was an all-star infielder. He suffered a career-ending shoulder surgery in 1973 at Class AA El Paso.[[14]](#cite_note-14) His father also played professional baseball in the minor leagues. His older sister, Jill, is the mother of former professional baseball player [Matt Franco](/wiki/Matt_Franco "Matt Franco").[[15]](#cite_note-15) From 1969 to 1975, Russell served in the [California Air National Guard](/wiki/California_Air_National_Guard "California Air National Guard") and belonged to the [146th Tactical Airlift Wing](/wiki/146th_Tactical_Airlift_Wing "146th Tactical Airlift Wing"), then based in [Van Nuys](/wiki/Van_Nuys "Van Nuys").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kurt_Russell&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### 1963–1979: Child actor with Disney and stardom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kurt_Russell&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1963–1979: Child actor with Disney and stardom")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Kurt_Russell_Robert_Vaughn_Man_From_UNCLE_1964.JPG/250px-Kurt_Russell_Robert_Vaughn_Man_From_UNCLE_1964.JPG)](/wiki/File:Kurt_Russell_Robert_Vaughn_Man_From_UNCLE_1964.JPG)

Russell with [Robert Vaughn](/wiki/Robert_Vaughn "Robert Vaughn") in a 1964 episode of *[The Man from U.N.C.L.E.](/wiki/The_Man_from_U.N.C.L.E. "The Man from U.N.C.L.E.")*

Russell made his film debut with an uncredited part for *[It Happened at the World's Fair](/wiki/It_Happened_at_the_World%27s_Fair "It Happened at the World's Fair")*, playing a boy who kicked a pilot ([Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley")) in the leg.[[17]](#cite_note-17) On April 24, 1963, Russell guest-starred in the ABC series *[Our Man Higgins](/wiki/Our_Man_Higgins "Our Man Higgins")*, starring [Stanley Holloway](/wiki/Stanley_Holloway "Stanley Holloway") as an English butler in an American family.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Later, he played the title role in the ABC [Western](/wiki/Western_(genre) "Western (genre)") series *[The Travels of Jaimie McPheeters](/wiki/The_Travels_of_Jaimie_McPheeters_(TV_series) "The Travels of Jaimie McPheeters (TV series)")* (1963–64). The show was based on [Robert Lewis Taylor](/wiki/Robert_Lewis_Taylor "Robert Lewis Taylor")'s eponymous novel, which [won the Pulitzer Prize for fiction](/wiki/The_Travels_of_Jaimie_McPheeters "The Travels of Jaimie McPheeters") in 1959.[[19]](#cite_note-19) In 1964, Russell guest-starred in "Nemesis", an episode of the ABC series *[The Fugitive](/wiki/The_Fugitive_(1963_TV_series) "The Fugitive (1963 TV series)")* in which, as the son of police Lt. Phillip Gerard, he is unintentionally kidnapped by his father's quarry, Doctor [Richard Kimble](/wiki/Richard_Kimble "Richard Kimble"). In [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC")'s *[The Virginian](/wiki/The_Virginian_(TV_series) "The Virginian (TV series)")*, he played the mistaken orphan whose father, played by [Rory Calhoun](/wiki/Rory_Calhoun "Rory Calhoun"), was an outlaw who was still alive and recently released from prison looking for his son.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Russell played a similar role as a kid named Packy Kerlin in the 1964 episode "Blue Heaven" for the Western series *[Gunsmoke](/wiki/Gunsmoke "Gunsmoke")*. At age 13, Russell played the role of Jungle Boy on an episode of [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS")'s *[Gilligan's Island](/wiki/Gilligan%27s_Island "Gilligan's Island")*, which aired on February 6, 1965.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In 1966, Russell was signed to a ten-year contract with [Walt Disney Productions](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Productions "Walt Disney Productions"), where he became, according to [Robert Osborne](/wiki/Robert_Osborne "Robert Osborne"), the "studio's top star of the '70s".[[22]](#cite_note-Osbourne2007-22) Russell's first film for Disney was *[Follow Me, Boys!](/wiki/Follow_Me,_Boys! "Follow Me, Boys!")* (1966). [Walt Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney "Walt Disney") described Russell as "a 15-year-old boy for whom I predict a great acting future",[[23]](#cite_note-23) in what would prove to be Disney's last filmed appearance before his death in December 1966. One of the last things Disney ever wrote was the name "Kurt Russell" (though misspelled as "Kirt") on a piece of paper.[[24]](#cite_note-24) In January 1967, Russell played Private Willie Prentiss in the episode "Willie and the Yank: The Mosby Raiders" in *[Walt Disney's Wonderful World of Color](/wiki/Walt_Disney%27s_Wonderful_World_of_Color "Walt Disney's Wonderful World of Color")*, released theatrically in some markets as *[Mosby's Marauders](/wiki/Mosby%27s_Marauders "Mosby's Marauders")* (1967). During this time, Russell continued to guest star on non-Disney TV shows. He, [Jay C. Flippen](/wiki/Jay_C._Flippen "Jay C. Flippen") and [Tom Tryon](/wiki/Tom_Tryon "Tom Tryon") appeared in the episode "Charade of Justice" of the NBC Western series *The Road West* starring [Barry Sullivan](/wiki/Barry_Sullivan_(American_actor) "Barry Sullivan (American actor)"). In a March 1966 episode of CBS's *[Lost in Space](/wiki/Lost_in_Space "Lost in Space")* entitled "The Challenge", he played Quano, the son of a planetary ruler.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Kurt_Russell_1974.JPG/250px-Kurt_Russell_1974.JPG)](/wiki/File:Kurt_Russell_1974.JPG)

Russell in a 1974 publicity photo

While filming the [Sherman Brothers](/wiki/Sherman_Brothers "Sherman Brothers") musical *[The One and Only, Genuine, Original Family Band](/wiki/The_One_and_Only,_Genuine,_Original_Family_Band "The One and Only, Genuine, Original Family Band")* (1968), Russell met his future partner [Goldie Hawn](/wiki/Goldie_Hawn "Goldie Hawn").[[26]](#cite_note-Kate-26) For Disney, he made *[The Horse in the Gray Flannel Suit](/wiki/The_Horse_in_the_Gray_Flannel_Suit "The Horse in the Gray Flannel Suit")* (1969) and *[Guns in the Heather](/wiki/Guns_in_the_Heather "Guns in the Heather")* (1969). Disney promoted Russell to star roles with *[The Computer Wore Tennis Shoes](/wiki/The_Computer_Wore_Tennis_Shoes "The Computer Wore Tennis Shoes")* (1969) which was a big hit. He followed it with *[The Barefoot Executive](/wiki/The_Barefoot_Executive "The Barefoot Executive")* (1971), another success. In 1971, he co-starred as a young robber released from jail alongside [James Stewart](/wiki/James_Stewart "James Stewart") in *[Fools' Parade](/wiki/Fools%27_Parade "Fools' Parade")*.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Later, he guest-starred in an episode of *[Room 222](/wiki/Room_222 "Room 222")* as an idealistic high school student who assumed the costumed identity of [Paul Revere](/wiki/Paul_Revere "Paul Revere") to warn of the dangers of pollution.[[28]](#cite_note-28) However, the bulk of his film work was for Disney in films such as *[Now You See Him, Now You Don't](/wiki/Now_You_See_Him,_Now_You_Don%27t "Now You See Him, Now You Don't")* (1971), *[Charley and the Angel](/wiki/Charley_and_the_Angel "Charley and the Angel")* (1973), and *[Superdad](/wiki/Superdad "Superdad")* (1973). In the autumn of 1974, he appeared in the ABC series *[The New Land](/wiki/The_New_Land_(TV_series) "The New Land (TV series)")*, inspired by the 1972 Swedish film of the [same name](/wiki/The_New_Land_(1972_film) "The New Land (1972 film)"). Critically acclaimed, it suffered very low ratings and aired only six of the 13 episodes. He returned to Disney for *[The Strongest Man in the World](/wiki/The_Strongest_Man_in_the_World "The Strongest Man in the World")* (1975).

### 1980–1999: Transition into Hollywood

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kurt_Russell&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1980–1999: Transition into Hollywood")]

Russell played the title character in the 1979 television film *[Elvis](/wiki/Elvis_(1979_film) "Elvis (1979 film)")*, in which his then-wife [Season Hubley](/wiki/Season_Hubley "Season Hubley") played [Priscilla](/wiki/Priscilla_Presley "Priscilla Presley").[[29]](#cite_note-29) This would transition Russell's Hollywood career after years as a child actor.[[30]](#cite_note-30) It was directed by [John Carpenter](/wiki/John_Carpenter "John Carpenter") and led to a series of collaborations between the two men. His performance earned him a nomination for the [Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Lead Actor in a Limited Series or Movie](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Lead_Actor_in_a_Limited_Series_or_Movie "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Lead Actor in a Limited Series or Movie").[[1]](#cite_note-Elvis-1) Russell starred in *Amber Waves* (1980) and the comedy *[Used Cars](/wiki/Used_Cars "Used Cars")* (1980). Russell then played the surly anti-hero [Snake Plissken](/wiki/Snake_Plissken "Snake Plissken") in the acclaimed [dystopian](/wiki/Dystopian "Dystopian") science fiction film, *[Escape from New York](/wiki/Escape_from_New_York "Escape from New York")* (1981), directed by Carpenter, in an image-changing opportunity Russell welcomed to escape his Disney typecasting.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

He returned to Disney to provide the voice of Copper as an adult for *[The Fox and the Hound](/wiki/The_Fox_and_the_Hound "The Fox and the Hound")* (1981) then reunited with Carpenter for *[The Thing](/wiki/The_Thing_(1982_film) "The Thing (1982 film)")* (1982), based upon the short story *[Who Goes There?](/wiki/Who_Goes_There%3F "Who Goes There?")* by [John W. Campbell, Jr.](/wiki/John_W._Campbell,_Jr. "John W. Campbell, Jr."), which had been interpreted on film before, albeit loosely, in 1951's *[The Thing from Another World](/wiki/The_Thing_from_Another_World "The Thing from Another World")*.[[32]](#cite_note-32) He was nominated for the [Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor – Motion Picture](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor_%E2%80%93_Motion_Picture "Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor – Motion Picture") for his performance in *[Silkwood](/wiki/Silkwood "Silkwood")* (1983).[[33]](#cite_note-33) Russell made *[Swing Shift](/wiki/Swing_Shift_(film) "Swing Shift (film)")* (1984) co-starring Goldie Hawn, who became his romantic partner. He starred in *[The Mean Season](/wiki/The_Mean_Season "The Mean Season")* (1985) and *[The Best of Times](/wiki/The_Best_of_Times_(1986_film) "The Best of Times (1986 film)")* (1986), then played an antihero truck driver caught in an ancient Chinese war in *[Big Trouble in Little China](/wiki/Big_Trouble_in_Little_China "Big Trouble in Little China")* (1986), another Carpenter film which, like *The Thing,* was initially a critical and commercial disappointment but has since gained a cult audience. More popular at the box office was *[Overboard](/wiki/Overboard_(1987_film) "Overboard (1987 film)")* (1987), a comedy with Goldie Hawn.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Russell credited his performance in *[Tequila Sunrise](/wiki/Tequila_Sunrise_(film) "Tequila Sunrise (film)")* (1988) with getting Hollywood to regard him differently.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] He starred in *[Winter People](/wiki/Winter_People "Winter People")* (1989) and co-starred with [Sylvester Stallone](/wiki/Sylvester_Stallone "Sylvester Stallone") in *[Tango & Cash](/wiki/Tango_%26_Cash "Tango & Cash")* (1989).

Russell followed this up by playing Lt. Stephen "Bull" McCaffrey in the drama *[Backdraft](/wiki/Backdraft_(film) "Backdraft (film)")* (1991), Michael Carr in the psychological thriller *[Unlawful Entry](/wiki/Unlawful_Entry_(film) "Unlawful Entry (film)")* (1992), a sailor in the comedy *[Captain Ron](/wiki/Captain_Ron "Captain Ron")* (1992), and [Colonel Jack O'Neil](/wiki/Jack_O%27Neill "Jack O'Neill") in the [military science fiction](/wiki/Military_science_fiction "Military science fiction") film *[Stargate](/wiki/Stargate_(film) "Stargate (film)")* (1994). He also played [Wyatt Earp](/wiki/Wyatt_Earp "Wyatt Earp") in the hit Western film *[Tombstone](/wiki/Tombstone_(film) "Tombstone (film)")* (1993). Russell later claimed in a 2006 interview that he had ghost-directed *Tombstone* on behalf of credited director [George P. Cosmatos](/wiki/George_P._Cosmatos "George P. Cosmatos"), saying he gave Cosmatos shot lists.[[35]](#cite_note-beck-35) Russell claimed Stallone recommended Cosmatos to him after the removal of the first director, writer [Kevin Jarre](/wiki/Kevin_Jarre "Kevin Jarre"), but Cosmatos had also worked with *Tombstone* executive producer [Andrew G. Vajna](/wiki/Andrew_G._Vajna "Andrew G. Vajna") before on *[Rambo: First Blood Part II](/wiki/Rambo:_First_Blood_Part_II "Rambo: First Blood Part II")*. Russell said he promised Cosmatos he would keep it a secret as long as Cosmatos was alive; Cosmatos died in April 2005.[[35]](#cite_note-beck-35) Russell said he did not get a chance to edit his version, but Vajna gave him a tape of "everything on the movie" and that he might try to "reconstruct the movie", although he would need to go back to the script and all his notes.[[35]](#cite_note-beck-35)

Rusell also had an uncredited role as the voice of [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley") in the 1994 film *[Forrest Gump](/wiki/Forrest_Gump "Forrest Gump")*.[[1]](#cite_note-Elvis-1) In 1996, Russell starred as a military intelligence consultant in the action film *[Executive Decision](/wiki/Executive_Decision "Executive Decision")*,[[36]](#cite_note-36) and again played Snake Plissken in Carpenter's follow-up to *Escape from New York*, *[Escape from L.A.](/wiki/Escape_from_L.A. "Escape from L.A.")*[[37]](#cite_note-37) He then starred as the husband of a kidnapped woman in the action thriller *[Breakdown](/wiki/Breakdown_(1997_film) "Breakdown (1997 film)")* (1997), and as a genetically enhanced soldier in the science fiction film *[Soldier](/wiki/Soldier_(1998_American_film) "Soldier (1998 American film)")* (1998).

### 2000–present: Franchise films and resurgence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kurt_Russell&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2000–present: Franchise films and resurgence")]

In 2001, Russell played a court psychologist in the thriller film *[Vanilla Sky](/wiki/Vanilla_Sky "Vanilla Sky")*. His portrayal of U.S. Olympic hockey coach [Herb Brooks](/wiki/Herb_Brooks "Herb Brooks") in the 2004 film *[Miracle](/wiki/Miracle_(2004_film) "Miracle (2004 film)")*, won the praise of critics. "In many ways", wrote Claudia Puig of *[USA Today](/wiki/USA_Today "USA Today")*, "*Miracle* belongs to Kurt Russell." [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") of the *[Chicago Sun-Times](/wiki/Chicago_Sun-Times "Chicago Sun-Times")* wrote, "Russell does real acting here."[[38]](#cite_note-38) [Elvis Mitchell](/wiki/Elvis_Mitchell "Elvis Mitchell") of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* wrote, "Mr. Russell's cagey and remote performance gives *Miracle* its few breezes of fresh air."[[39]](#cite_note-39) In 2005, he played a horse trainer father in the family sports film *[Dreamer](/wiki/Dreamer_(2005_film) "Dreamer (2005 film)")* and a father with superhuman strength in the superhero film *[Sky High](/wiki/Sky_High_(2005_film) "Sky High (2005 film)")*.

Russell starred in [Wolfgang Petersen](/wiki/Wolfgang_Petersen "Wolfgang Petersen")'s *[Poseidon](/wiki/Poseidon_(film) "Poseidon (film)")* (2006).[[40]](#cite_note-40) He also played the villainous Stuntman Mike in [Quentin Tarantino](/wiki/Quentin_Tarantino "Quentin Tarantino")'s segment *[Death Proof](/wiki/Death_Proof "Death Proof")* of the film *[Grindhouse](/wiki/Grindhouse_(film) "Grindhouse (film)")* (2007), and was in two more Tarantino films, *[The Hateful Eight](/wiki/The_Hateful_Eight "The Hateful Eight")* (2015) and *[Once Upon a Time in Hollywood](/wiki/Once_Upon_a_Time_in_Hollywood "Once Upon a Time in Hollywood")* (2019). After a remake of *Escape from New York* was announced, Russell was reportedly upset that [Gerard Butler](/wiki/Gerard_Butler "Gerard Butler") was attached to play his signature character, Snake Plissken, as he believed the character "was quintessentially [...] American."[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) Russell appeared in *[The Battered Bastards of Baseball](/wiki/The_Battered_Bastards_of_Baseball "The Battered Bastards of Baseball")*, a documentary about his father and the Portland Mavericks, which debuted at the [Sundance Film Festival](/wiki/Sundance_Film_Festival "Sundance Film Festival") in 2014.[[43]](#cite_note-43) In 2015, Russell garnered attention for his portrayal of Sheriff Franklin Hunt in the Western-horror film *[Bone Tomahawk](/wiki/Bone_Tomahawk "Bone Tomahawk")*.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)

During this time, Russell also appeared in the *[Fast & Furious](/wiki/Fast_%26_Furious "Fast & Furious")* franchise playing Mr. Nobody, starring in three of its films, *[Furious 7](/wiki/Furious_7 "Furious 7")* (2015), *[The Fate of the Furious](/wiki/The_Fate_of_the_Furious "The Fate of the Furious")* (2017), and *[F9](/wiki/F9_(film) "F9 (film)")* (2021).[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[2]](#cite_note-Taylor-2) In 2016, he starred as Jimmy Harrell in *[Deepwater Horizon](/wiki/Deepwater_Horizon_(film) "Deepwater Horizon (film)")*, a film about the [2010 *Deepwater Horizon* oil spill](/wiki/Deepwater_Horizon_oil_spill "Deepwater Horizon oil spill").[[47]](#cite_note-RussellCast-47) On May 4, 2017, Russell and Goldie Hawn received stars in a double star ceremony on the [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame") for their achievements in motion pictures, located at 6201 [Hollywood Boulevard](/wiki/Hollywood_Boulevard "Hollywood Boulevard").[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50) In 2017 he played [Star-Lord](/wiki/Star-Lord "Star-Lord")'s father Ego in the [Marvel Cinematic Universe](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe "Marvel Cinematic Universe") film *[Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2 "Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2")* (2017). Todd McCarthy of *[The Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter")* praised the chemistry between [Chris Pratt](/wiki/Chris_Pratt "Chris Pratt") and Russell writing, "the scenes between the well-cast Russell and Pratt are the best in the film".[[51]](#cite_note-51) Russell played [Santa Claus](/wiki/Santa_Claus "Santa Claus") in the [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") films *[The Christmas Chronicles](/wiki/The_Christmas_Chronicles "The Christmas Chronicles")* (2018) and *[The Christmas Chronicles 2](/wiki/The_Christmas_Chronicles_2 "The Christmas Chronicles 2")* (2020).[[52]](#cite_note-TCC2-52) From 2021 to 2023, he reprised his role as Ego in the MCU [Disney+](/wiki/Disney%2B "Disney+") animated series *[What If...?](/wiki/What_If...%3F_(TV_series) "What If...? (TV series)")* voicing the role in the episodes "[What If... T'Challa Became a Star-Lord?](/wiki/What_If..._T%27Challa_Became_a_Star-Lord%3F "What If... T'Challa Became a Star-Lord?")", "[What If... the Watcher Broke His Oath?](/wiki/What_If..._the_Watcher_Broke_His_Oath%3F "What If... the Watcher Broke His Oath?")" and "[What If... Peter Quill Attacked Earth's Mightiest Heroes?](/wiki/What_If..._Peter_Quill_Attacked_Earth%27s_Mightiest_Heroes%3F "What If... Peter Quill Attacked Earth's Mightiest Heroes?")". In 2025, it was announced that Russell would join the cast of the *[Yellowstone](/wiki/Yellowstone_(TV_series) "Yellowstone (TV series)")* spin-off *[The Madison](/wiki/The_Madison_(TV_series) "The Madison (TV series)")*.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

## Other ventures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kurt_Russell&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Other ventures")]

### Baseball career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kurt_Russell&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Baseball career")]

Russell, like his father, had a baseball career. In the early 1970s, Russell was a [switch-hitting](/wiki/Switch-hitting "Switch-hitting") [second baseman](/wiki/Second_baseman "Second baseman") for the [California Angels](/wiki/California_Angels "California Angels") minor league affiliates, the [Bend Rainbows](/wiki/Bend_Rainbows "Bend Rainbows") (1971)[[54]](#cite_note-atpfrb-54)[[55]](#cite_note-rbdetntf-55)[[56]](#cite_note-irwodsn-56)[[57]](#cite_note-swkrw-57) and [Walla Walla](/wiki/Walla_Walla,_Washington "Walla Walla, Washington") Islanders (1972) in the [short season](/wiki/Short_season "Short season") [Class A-Short Season](/wiki/Class_A-Short_Season "Class A-Short Season") [Northwest League](/wiki/Northwest_League "Northwest League"),[[58]](#cite_note-msssc-58)[[59]](#cite_note-rucab-59) then moved up to Class [AA](/wiki/Double-A_(baseball) "Double-A (baseball)") in 1973 with the [El Paso Sun Kings](/wiki/El_Paso_Sun_Kings#The_dawn_of_the_Sun_Kings "El Paso Sun Kings") of the [Texas League](/wiki/Texas_League "Texas League").[[60]](#cite_note-bdtmg-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)

While Russell was in the field turning the pivot of a [double play](/wiki/Double_play "Double play") early in the season, the incoming runner at second base collided with him and tore the [rotator cuff](/wiki/Rotator_cuff "Rotator cuff") in Russell's right (throwing) shoulder. He did not return to El Paso, but was a [designated hitter](/wiki/Designated_hitter "Designated hitter") for the independent [Portland Mavericks](/wiki/Portland_Mavericks "Portland Mavericks") in the Northwest League late in their short season. The team was owned by his father, and he had been doing promotional work for them in the interim.[[62]](#cite_note-wkrtjmros-62) The injury forced his retirement from baseball in 1973 and led to his return to acting.[[63]](#cite_note-bpkr-63)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kurt_Russell&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Personal life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/KurtRussellSept2013TIFF.jpg/250px-KurtRussellSept2013TIFF.jpg)](/wiki/File:KurtRussellSept2013TIFF.jpg)

Russell at the [2013 Toronto International Film Festival](/wiki/2013_Toronto_International_Film_Festival "2013 Toronto International Film Festival")

Russell married actress [Season Hubley](/wiki/Season_Hubley "Season Hubley"), whom he met while filming *Elvis*, in 1979, and they had a son, Boston (born February 16, 1980). After his divorce from Hubley in 1983, Russell began his relationship with [Goldie Hawn](/wiki/Goldie_Hawn "Goldie Hawn"), and appeared alongside her in *Swing Shift* and *Overboard*, having previously appeared with her in *The One and Only, Genuine, Original Family Band* in 1968. They have a son, [Wyatt Russell](/wiki/Wyatt_Russell "Wyatt Russell") (born July 10, 1986), and have owned homes in [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"), [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"),[[64]](#cite_note-NYT_At_Home_With-64)[[26]](#cite_note-Kate-26) [Snowmass Village, Colorado](/wiki/Snowmass_Village,_Colorado "Snowmass Village, Colorado"),[[65]](#cite_note-auto1-65) [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), New York,[[66]](#cite_note-auto2-66) [Brentwood](/wiki/Brentwood,_California "Brentwood, California"),[[67]](#cite_note-auto3-67) and [Palm Desert, California](/wiki/Palm_Desert,_California "Palm Desert, California").[[68]](#cite_note-auto4-68) In an interview with [*People*](/wiki/People_(magazine) "People (magazine)") in December 2020, Russell revealed that he and Hawn never felt the need to marry, stating that a "marriage certificate wasn't going to create anything that otherwise we wouldn't have."[[69]](#cite_note-69)

Russell is a [libertarian](/wiki/Libertarian "Libertarian").[[70]](#cite_note-70) In 2020, he stated that celebrities should keep their political opinions to themselves, believing that it negatively impacts their work.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

Russell is a hunter and a staunch supporter of [gun rights](/wiki/Gun_rights "Gun rights"), and said that gun control will not reduce terrorism.[[72]](#cite_note-72) He is also an FAA-licensed private pilot holding single/multi-engine and [instrument ratings](/wiki/Instrument_rating "Instrument rating"), and is an Honorary Council Member of the humanitarian aviation organization [Wings of Hope](/wiki/Wings_of_Hope_(charity) "Wings of Hope (charity)").[[73]](#cite_note-73) In 2010, he was inducted as part of the [Living Legends of Aviation](/wiki/Living_Legends_of_Aviation "Living Legends of Aviation"), receiving the "Aviation Mentor Award" from fellow actor-pilot [John Travolta](/wiki/John_Travolta "John Travolta").[[74]](#cite_note-74)

In February 2003, Russell and Hawn moved to [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"), [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia") so that their son could play ice hockey.[[64]](#cite_note-NYT_At_Home_With-64)
